You could do 10%, 15%, 25%. And then we're going to multiply that by the expected return and then bring that up to a power of two. So, more

title: How to Test a Trading Strategy & Build a Trade Log
text: And then we're going to divide that by a margin of error. So, this is using a 15% margin of error. You could do 10%, 15%, 25%. And then we're going to multiply that by the expected return and then bring that up to a power of two. So, more often than not, what you're going to notice is that the required trades is going to be a huge number. But it doesn't mean that whatever you're doing is useless unless you hit this number. What it means is that you need to track your progress. So, what that means is if I'm trading the strategy, let's say it's something that needs 10,000 trades. That doesn't mean I'm going to wait till 10,000 trades. What it means is that as I'm trading it, I'm going to observe the performance. And is the required number of trades calculated? Is it getting bigger or is it contracting? Because if it's contracting, that means that what I'm doing is tightening up the consistency of this thing and I'm moving in the right direction.
